topic: A person finds their childhood home listed for sale online
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the screen, her thumb hovering over the refresh button. The listing was stark: "14 Willow Creek Lane – Charming Bungalow – Priced to Sell." A slow, heavy thud resonated in her chest, a feeling like a sudden drop in temperature. She traced the faded photograph of the front porch, the one where she used to hide from summer rain. *This can’t be right.* Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She reread the address, the familiar, slightly crooked mailbox on the corner, the scent of old lilac bushes that still clung to the air in her memory. A strange tension tightened her jaw. She stood up abruptly, pacing the length of her cramped living room, her footsteps echoing unnaturally loud on the hardwood floor. Why now? After all this time, why this sudden, jarring intrusion?

[story 2]

Mark leaned back in his worn office chair, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead seeming to intensify the quiet anxiety radiating from him. His eyes flickered between the screen displaying the property details and the framed diploma on his wall. The listing for his childhood address—the house his parents had built, the place where scraped knees healed and secrets were whispered—was undeniable. He picked up his phone, his fingers trembling slightly as he navigated to the agent's contact information. He didn't call. Instead, he just held the device, his knuckles white against the cool glass. A knot formed just beneath his sternum, constricting his breathing. He squeezed his eyes shut for a long moment, trying to anchor himself, but the image of the peeling blue paint on the porch seemed to swim behind his eyelids. He opened them, his gaze fixed on the text, a strange, hollow emptiness settling over him.

[story 3]

“No. Absolutely not.” Sarah muttered, tapping furiously on her tablet, the sound sharp in the otherwise silent apartment. The listing for her first home, the sanctuary of her youth, was front and center on the screen. She leaned forward, resting her elbows on her knees, her posture rigid. A dizzying sensation washed over her; the floor seemed to tilt slightly. She ran a hand through her hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically pull the reality away. Every detail—the overgrown rose garden, the specific angle of the window in the kitchen—was painfully vivid, yet utterly alien. She swallowed hard, the dryness in her throat making the action difficult. She took a deep, shaky breath, her gaze darting back and forth across the listing, unable to settle on any single point.
